input.content:
# Executor Registry — Node-Type Mapping

## Context

Question: how does typeflow's executor registry map workflow node types to their executor functions?

## Answer

- **File:** `server/services/executor-registry.ts`
- **Registry class:** `ExecutorRegistry` (defined at line 52, singleton exported at line 86 as `executorRegistry`)
- **Registration methods** (three, depending on node behavior):
  - `register(nodeType, executor)` — line 57 — for normal data-transforming nodes (e.g. `filter`, `openai`, `aiAgent`, `code`)
  - `registerConditional(nodeType, executor)` — line 61 — for branching nodes (`if`, `switch`) that route items to multiple outputs
  - `registerPassThrough(nodeType)` — line 65 — for nodes that just forward input (`trigger`, `webhook`, `noop`, `manualTrigger`, `scheduleTrigger`, `chatTrigger`, `workflowTrigger`)

## How the mapping works

Internally the registry holds three collections, all keyed by the node `type` string:

```ts
private executors           = new Map<string, NodeExecutor>();
private conditionalExecutors = new Map<string, ConditionalNodeExecutor>();
private passThroughTypes    = new Set<string>();
```

`register("openai", { execute: async (node, inputItems, ctx) => executeOpenAINode(...) })` stores a thin `NodeExecutor` adapter that calls into the per-domain executor module (`ai-executor.ts`, `database-executor.ts`, `data-transform-executor.ts`, etc.). Lookup at runtime is `executorRegistry.get(node.type)` / `.getConditional(node.type)` / `.isPassThrough(node.type)`.

All built-in registrations happen at module load time at the bottom of the same file (lines 88–323).

## Caveat worth noting

The file header says the registry "replaces the monolithic if/else chain in workflow-executor.ts" — but `server/services/workflow-executor.ts` still contains that if/else chain (lines 234–340 in `executeUntilNode`, lines 478–706 in `executeWorkflow`, lines 1117–1199 in `executeWithDebug`). The registry exists and is fully populated, but the main dispatch loop hasn't been migrated to use it yet. So the registry is the *intended* mapping mechanism; the *current* dispatch is still hardcoded.

This is a research question, not an implementation plan, so I'll answer directly rather than calling ExitPlanMode.

**File:** `server/services/executor-registry.ts`
**Registry class:** `ExecutorRegistry` (line 52; singleton exported as `executorRegistry` on line 86)
**Registration method:** `register(nodeType: string, executor: NodeExecutor)` (line 57)

It's actually three methods, depending on the node's behavior:

| Method | Line | Used for |
|---|---|---|
| `register(type, executor)` | 57 | Normal nodes (`filter`, `openai`, `aiAgent`, `code`, `httpRequest`, …) |
| `registerConditional(type, executor)` | 61 | Branching nodes that route items to multiple outputs (`if`, `switch`) |
| `registerPassThrough(type)` | 65 | Nodes that forward input unchanged (`trigger`, `webhook`, `noop`, `manualTrigger`, `scheduleTrigger`, `chatTrigger`, `workflowTrigger`) |

Internally each method writes to one of three collections keyed by the node `type` string:

```ts
private executors            = new Map<string, NodeExecutor>();
private conditionalExecutors = new Map<string, ConditionalNodeExecutor>();
private passThroughTypes     = new Set<string>();
```

All built-in registrations happen at module load time at the bottom of the same file (lines 132–323), each one wrapping a per-domain executor function from `ai-executor.ts`, `database-executor.ts`, `data-transform-executor.ts`, etc.

**One caveat worth flagging:** the file's header comment says it "replaces the monolithic if/else chain in workflow-executor.ts" — but `server/services/workflow-executor.ts` still contains that if/else chain in all three execution paths (`executeUntilNode`, `executeWorkflow`, `executeWithDebug`). The registry is fully populated but the main dispatch loop hasn't been migrated to use it yet, so it's the *intended* mapping mechanism, not the *active* one.

This is a research question, not an implementation plan, so I'll answer directly rather than calling ExitPlanMode.

**File:** `server/services/executor-registry.ts`
**Registry class:** `ExecutorRegistry` (line 52; singleton exported as `executorRegistry` on line 86)
**Registration method:** `register(nodeType: string, executor: NodeExecutor)` (line 57)

It's actually three methods, depending on the node's behavior:

| Method | Line | Used for |
|---|---|---|
| `register(type, executor)` | 57 | Normal nodes (`filter`, `openai`, `aiAgent`, `code`, `httpRequest`, …) |
| `registerConditional(type, executor)` | 61 | Branching nodes that route items to multiple outputs (`if`, `switch`) |
| `registerPassThrough(type)` | 65 | Nodes that forward input unchanged (`trigger`, `webhook`, `noop`, `manualTrigger`, `scheduleTrigger`, `chatTrigger`, `workflowTrigger`) |

Internally each method writes to one of three collections keyed by the node `type` string:

```ts
private executors            = new Map<string, NodeExecutor>();
private conditionalExecutors = new Map<string, ConditionalNodeExecutor>();
private passThroughTypes     = new Set<string>();
```

All built-in registrations happen at module load time at the bottom of the same file (lines 132–323), each one wrapping a per-domain executor function from `ai-executor.ts`, `database-executor.ts`, `data-transform-executor.ts`, etc.

**One caveat worth flagging:** the file's header comment says it "replaces the monolithic if/else chain in workflow-executor.ts" — but `server/services/workflow-executor.ts` still contains that if/else chain in all three execution paths (`executeUntilNode`, `executeWorkflow`, `executeWithDebug`). The registry is fully populated but the main dispatch loop hasn't been migrated to use it yet, so it's the *intended* mapping mechanism, not the *active* one.
